questions.specification:
What is keeping this structure in equilibrium?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXf3wOZk_3dJjMTaSa-WRDUNx41Y3-pvqGbynjKzb8t287lskBccTIC7dOGjqHsmoTg-Am6RL61bzICnLnVRr8ogzU3hPxsTTbhuSkin_bTERchXG74vaA_soYLpoeRdCpXZAC9kAg?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
Why are structures often made up of multiple interconnected components?

options.content: It helps optimize load distribution and allows modular construction
It reduces the material cost per component

questions.specification:
Why might materials with different stiffness be used in this product?
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ef96747a-6865-40d2-b60d-011bb65d99b3)

options.content: They reduce vibration and improve performance
options.markscheme: Materials with different stiffness can be combined to tune stiffness and damping, reducing unwanted vibration while maintaining required support.

options.content: They provide flexibility in some areas while keeping other areas rigid for support
options.markscheme: This is a plausible reason for using different stiffness, but it does not best match the vibration/performance focus indicated by the product context compared with option A.

options.content: They help distribute loads more evenly and reduce stress concentrations at joints
options.markscheme: Using regions of different stiffness can reduce stress concentrations, but this is not the most direct/primary reason compared with vibration reduction and performance tuning in this product.

options.content: They increase comfort by allowing controlled deformation while maintaining overall stability
options.markscheme: Different stiffness can improve comfort through controlled compliance, but the best answer here is vibration reduction and performance improvement (option A).

questions.specification:
FIA Formula 1 regulations require a carbon-fibre monocoque survival cell designed to protect the driver during high-energy crashes. Mercedes-AMG Petronas Formula One Team develops chassis that must be extremely stiff for handling, as light as possible for performance, and compliant with strict crash tests and structural rules.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6856c2af-24a2-413b-b2be-d22d0a359738)

parts.content: Outline why torsional rigidity is important for load-bearing structural frames.
parts.markscheme:
- Torsional rigidity refers to the ability of a structure to resist twisting or deformation when subjected to torque or moments. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High torsional rigidity is essential to maintain suspension geometry and wheel alignment under high loads, ensuring predictable handling and vehicle stability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how space frame and monocoque construction methods produce different performance characteristics in racing chassis.
parts.markscheme:
- A space frame uses a network of interconnected members working in tension and compression, offering a design that is relatively easy to repair and modify. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monocoque construction uses an external skin to carry the primary loads, providing a significantly higher strength-to-weight ratio and torsional stiffness than space frames. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The integrated nature of the monocoque shell allows for a more compact and aerodynamic survival cell, which enhances both driver safety and vehicle speed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While space frames are heavier and less stiff, their simpler construction methods make them more cost-effective for lower-tier racing compared to expensive composite monocoques. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how crash safety requirements influence the structural design of racing vehicle chassis.
parts.markscheme:
- Safety regulations necessitate the design of crumple zones, which are structural components engineered to deform in a controlled manner to absorb kinetic energy during an impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The driver compartment must be designed as a high-strength survival cell that resists penetration and maintains structural integrity to prevent injury. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Material selection is influenced by impact performance, where composites like carbon-Kevlar are used for their ability to dissipate energy without brittle failure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers must map specific load paths to ensure that impact forces are directed away from the driver and into secondary structures like the engine block or transmission. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mandatory roll hoops and side-impact structures must be integrated into the chassis, which dictates the overall geometry and mass distribution of the vehicle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- There is an analytical trade-off between the absolute stiffness required for aerodynamic efficiency and the compliance needed for energy management in a crash. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the design optimisation process used to balance weight, strength, stiffness, and safety in racing chassis design.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative points:

- **Weight vs Structural Performance:** Reducing mass is critical for acceleration and cornering, but designers must ensure that stripping material does not lower the factor of safety or reduce torsional stiffness below acceptable levels.
- **Strength and Load Capacity:** The chassis must survive peak loads during heavy braking and high-speed cornering; this sets a floor for material thickness and dictates where reinforcement (e.g., localized plies of carbon fibre) must be added.
- **Stiffness and Handling:** High torsional rigidity is non-negotiable for suspension performance, yet the stiffest designs can often be the heaviest, requiring iterative FEA (Finite Element Analysis) to remove material from non-load-bearing areas.
- **Crash Safety Constraints:** Safety structures add significant mass and may compromise aerodynamics, forcing designers to optimise the survival cell to be as lean as possible while still passing rigorous FIA static and dynamic tests.
- **Material Optimisation (Anisotropy):** Advanced composites allow for directional optimisation, where fibres are aligned with specific load paths to maximise strength and stiffness while minimising redundant material.
- **Simulation and Iteration:** The use of digital twin technology and crash simulations allows for rapid prototyping, enabling designers to explore the limits of material performance before physical manufacturing.

**Marking Criteria:**

<marks>1–2 marks</marks>
Limited evaluation. Responses identify one or two factors such as weight or safety with little explanation and minimal consideration of trade-offs.

<marks>3–4 marks</marks>
Some evaluation. Several factors are described with basic reasoning, but the balancing process between competing requirements is weak or uneven.

<marks>5–6 marks</marks>
Clear evaluation. Responses explain multiple competing design requirements and show a sound understanding of how optimisation involves trade-offs between weight, strength, stiffness, and safety.

<marks>7–8 marks</marks>
Thorough, well-balanced evaluation. Responses consistently analyse the optimisation process, justify trade-offs using structural reasoning, and show clear understanding of how performance, safety, and efficiency are balanced within regulatory and practical constraints.

questions.specification: A designer labels a structure as “frame” rather than “solid.” What type of classification is this?
Physics HL Paper 2

parts.content: Explain how safety factors are applied in structural design and why values above 1 are common.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** the safety factor ($SF$) as the ratio of ultimate strength to required strength: $SF = \dfrac{\text{ultimate strength}}{\text{required strength}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that safety factors are applied to provide a margin of safety against structural failure under unforeseen or extreme conditions <marks>R1</marks>

- **Describe** the application through an example; for instance, a safety factor of $SF = 2$ implies the structure is built to carry twice the maximum expected load <marks>A1</marks>

- **State** reasons for values greater than 1, such as uncertainties in load estimates, material defects, or degradation over time <marks>A1</marks>

- **Identify** examples of structural systems where these are applied, such as bridges, lifts, or aerospace structures <marks>A1</marks>

- **Outline** the consequences of a safety factor where $SF < 1$, which indicates that the structure is likely to fail under the intended design loads <marks>R1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award marks for accuracy and demonstration of applied understanding of safety factors in engineering contexts.</note>

questions.specification: A design consultancy is developing three types of public-use structures. These include a cantilevered balcony system, a modular pedestrian bridge, and a public art canopy. Each design prioritizes different structural principles for stability, safety, and aesthetics.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a cantilevered steel balcony protruding from a modern building façade.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/11837ae2-aa30-4773-8cb7-4a1756f49368)

i.) Identify one structural force acting on the cantilevered balcony.
parts.markscheme:
- Shear force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tension <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compression <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bending (bending moment) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Explain one way in which cantilevers resist the force identified in part (i).
parts.markscheme:
- States a correct resisting mechanism linked to the named force (e.g., shear is carried by the beam’s shear capacity and the vertical reaction at the fixed support; bending is resisted by an internal bending moment developed at the fixed end) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how the fixed support and member action counteract the applied load (e.g., the fixed end provides a vertical reaction to balance load and an opposing moment to prevent rotation/deflection) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a temporary modular pedestrian bridge made from prefabricated trusses.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8adfae4d-3e63-454b-b091-779de3fa7b64) 


i.) Explain one advantage of using a truss structure for the modular bridge.
parts.markscheme: - Trusses distribute loads efficiently through interconnected triangles, minimizing material use while maintaining strength. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Describe how the shape of a truss provides rigidity.
parts.markscheme: - The triangular arrangement of members in a truss prevents deformation under loads by maintaining fixed angles, creating geometric stability. <marks>3 marks</marks>

parts.content:
Figure 3 shows a large public canopy made from a grid shell structure supported by tension cables.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d1d435ce-8966-4de2-bb1d-e9c3b4fb43f7)

i.) Outline two reasons why tension members are suitable for the canopy.
parts.markscheme:
- Tension members are lightweight, reducing overall dead load. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow long spans with slender elements/cables, helping limit sag/deflection when properly tensioned. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They efficiently resist pulling forces, suitable where the form directs loads into tension. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They can accommodate curved/free-form geometries while remaining efficient. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any two valid outlined reasons linked to tension performance and canopy requirements. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: ii.) Compare the structural behavior of the grid shell canopy with a rigid frame structure in public architecture.
parts.markscheme:
- Grid shells transfer loads via their double-curved surfaces, allowing for aesthetic and efficient form-finding. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rigid frames rely on straight beams and columns to resist vertical and lateral loads. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Grid shells are better at spanning large open areas with fewer supports, while rigid frames require more frequent structural interruptions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, rigid frames are simpler to design and fabricate than complex shells. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Grid shells use material curvature and tension/compression balance, while rigid frames emphasize joint stiffness. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, grid shells offer lightweight aesthetics, whereas rigid frames offer construction speed and predictability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which factor most influences the choice of Young modulus when selecting a material for a structure?

options.content: The amount of deformation allowed under load
options.markscheme: Young modulus is a measure of the stiffness of a material. In structural design, a material is chosen with a specific Young modulus to ensure that the resulting deformation (strain) under the expected loads (stress) remains within acceptable limits.

options.content: The number of moving parts in a system
options.markscheme: The number of moving parts relates to the mechanical complexity and maintenance of a system, but it does not directly dictate the required Young modulus of the materials used.
While environmental impact is an important consideration in material selection, it is a sustainability factor rather than a mechanical property like Young modulus.
Surface finishing cost is a manufacturing consideration related to aesthetics or corrosion resistance, not to the bulk stiffness (Young modulus) of the material.

questions.specification:
Which real-world example illustrates the ubiquity of structures in both nature and human environments?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdWN6KbKDosNZoajKLA3t4qJKpHQBXKI5KHwVFSYSR5qnT_IQ0u-4DAewFaQIA6ILR7tY128Ly-rGgopnnG_Go4e0QOEw0VQddHiIOmjsAqEPP9r0Z1QrMdmQnk6NzcSBXUHgOxlQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
A structure experiencing constant gravitational force is subject to what kind of load?
Which material characteristic determines how much it will stretch under a given load?
A structure begins to buckle when a certain load is applied. This suggests:

options.content: The compressive load has exceeded the critical buckling load for the member.
options.markscheme: Buckling occurs when a slender member under compression reaches/exceeds its critical buckling load (loss of stability).

options.content: The material's hardness is too high.
options.markscheme: Hardness is a surface/material property and is not the determining factor for elastic buckling of a member under compression.

options.content: The safety factor is too large.
options.markscheme: A larger safety factor means more margin against failure; buckling would be associated with an insufficient safety factor (too small).
An overbuilt structure has more capacity than required; buckling suggests insufficient stiffness/capacity for the applied compressive load.

parts.content: Analyse the relationship between material selection (based on Young's modulus) and structural stability in high-load applications.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that high-load applications require materials with a high Young's modulus ($E$) to provide high stiffness <marks>A1</marks>

- **Recall** the relationship $E = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}}$ (or $E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$) <marks>M1</marks>

- **Analyse** that a higher $E$ value results in smaller elastic strain for a given applied stress <marks>R1</marks>

- **Explain** that minimal deformation is crucial for maintaining structural geometry and preventing failure modes like buckling or misalignment <marks>R1</marks>

- **Describe** the necessity of operating within the linear elastic region of the stress-strain curve to ensure reversible deformation <marks>A1</marks>

- **Contrast** materials using examples, such as steel ($E \approx 210\text{ GPa}$) for structural frames versus polymers ($E \approx 1\text{--}5\text{ GPa}$) which lack sufficient stability for high loads <marks>A1</marks>

- **Discuss** the limitation that high stiffness can sometimes be associated with brittleness, requiring a balance with material toughness <marks>R1</marks>

- **Explain** how composite materials allow for high specific modulus (ratio of $E$ to density) to optimize performance for moving or suspended loads <marks>R1</marks>

- **Conclude** that material choice balances $E$ with safety factors and environmental conditions to ensure overall structural integrity <marks>A1</marks>

9 marks total

<note>Award full marks for critical thinking and applied analysis that successfully links material properties to specific structural consequences.</note>

parts.content: Define a cantilever beam and explain its typical use in structural design.
parts.markscheme:
- State the definition of a cantilever beam as a beam anchored at only one end with the other end projecting freely <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify a typical use in structural design, such as in balconies, overhangs, or bridges <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for a correct partial definition or context.</note>

questions.specification:
The following diagram shows a suspension bridge swaying and twisting under the influence of strong, steady winds.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/9eeb07f9-8232-403a-8f8f-b0c92addc985.jpeg)

What type of force is primarily responsible for the swaying and oscillatory motion shown?

options.markscheme: Incorrect. A static force is a constant load that does not change over time and would not cause the bridge to oscillate or sway.
Incorrect. Compressive forces act to squeeze or shorten a structural member. While present within the bridge's components, they do not describe the nature of the external wind load causing motion.

Dynamic forces are loads that vary in magnitude, direction, or point of application over time. The interaction between the wind and the structure (aeroelastic flutter) creates time-dependent forces that cause the observed swaying and twisting oscillations. <marks>A1</marks>

Incorrect. Shear forces act parallel to the cross-section of a material. While internal shear stresses are generated during the swaying, 'dynamic force' is the correct classification for the time-varying load responsible for the motion.

questions.specification: The question assesses understanding of the three primary structural types in product design (frame, shell, and solid) and the differences in how they manage loads and enclose space.

parts.content: Explain the differences between frame, shell, and solid structures using product examples.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** a frame structure as a network of rigid members joined together to support loads (e.g., a bicycle frame or a crane jib). <marks>A1</marks>
- **Define** a shell structure as a structure with a thin, curved outer surface that encloses a volume and distributes loads across its skin (e.g., a car body or a soda can). <marks>A1</marks>
- **Define** a solid structure as a structure made entirely of one piece of material or a dense mass that relies on its weight and volume for strength (e.g., a concrete dam or a solid wooden bench). <marks>A1</marks>
- **Contrast** the three types by explaining that frame structures distribute loads through specific internal members, shell structures through their surface geometry, and solid structures through their entire mass. <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each type correctly explained with a relevant product example. Award the final mark for a clear comparison of how they differ in structural behavior.</note>

questions.specification:
Which characteristic of structural design is highlighted in the diagram?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/679fca7f-2663-4151-916f-c88efe017247.jpeg)

options.content: Use of multiple parts to increase strength
options.markscheme: Correct. Laminated timber (Glulam) consists of multiple layers (lamellae) bonded together. This construction method allows for the creation of structural members that are stronger and more stable than a single solid piece of wood of the same size, as defects in individual layers are dispersed.
Incorrect. A monolithic form refers to a structure made from a single, continuous piece of material. The diagram clearly shows a composite or laminated structure made of distinct layers.
Incorrect. While lamination can have an aesthetic appeal, the primary structural design characteristic illustrated is the use of layers to enhance mechanical properties.
Incorrect. Load estimation is a phase of the design process involving calculations or simulations; it is not a physical characteristic of the structural design shown in the physical object.

questions.specification: Designers are increasingly drawing inspiration from nature to develop stronger and more efficient structural systems that perform under stress while minimising material use.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a termite mound, a naturally formed structure with internal channels that help regulate airflow and temperature.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ffa25d62-75a9-4e20-965c-c0332c5857ef)

Refer to **Figure 1**. Identify the type of structure it represents and state one benefit it offers in terms of environmental control.
parts.markscheme:
- Solid structure <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Internal ventilation regulates temperature without external energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for structure classification, [1] for relevant environmental benefit*

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 1**. Explain how mimicking natural structures like termite mounds can influence modern architectural design.
parts.markscheme:
- Bio-inspired designs improve energy efficiency in buildings <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Encourages passive ventilation systems <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Reduces reliance on mechanical cooling and materials <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid point up to [3 max]*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a metal observation tower with a tubular frame, designed to handle torsional and bending forces in high winds.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e56f6f26-717a-4ba8-9501-cbb5914268dc)

Describe the different types of forces acting on the tower and how the structure resists them.
parts.markscheme:
- Wind applies torsional and bending forces <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Tubular frame distributes loads evenly <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Structural cross-bracing resists twist and deformation <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Anchored base resists vertical compression <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per correct description up to [4 max]*

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 2**. Using your understanding of structural design, explain how the tower is built to withstand forces higher than its expected load.
parts.markscheme:
- Incorporates a safety factor above 1 for extreme weather conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Material selection (e.g. steel alloys) enhances strength-to-weight ratio <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Dynamic forces anticipated with flexibility zones in joints or supports <marks>1 mark</marks>


- Excess strength reduces failure risk and extends longevity <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per appropriate explanation up to [4 max]*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a carbon-fibre bike frame designed to flex under controlled loads while maintaining high tensile strength.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/872a9509-6d4e-46cb-bc4b-e511e21c55dc)

Refer to **Figure 3**. Discuss the relationship between stress and strain in the bike frame and how it is optimised through material choice.
parts.markscheme:
- Carbon fibre has high tensile strength and low weight <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Stress–strain curve allows elastic deformation under load <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Minimises permanent deformation (high yield strength) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Ultimate strength ensures durability under extreme loads <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Stress is absorbed and distributed through composite layering <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Maintains performance while preventing breakage <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid point up to [6 max]*

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 3**. Compare two different materials by their Young’s Modulus and explain how the bike’s material choice supports performance goals.
parts.markscheme:
- Carbon fibre: high Young’s Modulus → stiff under load <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Aluminium: lower Young’s Modulus → more flex, less precision <marks>1 mark</marks>

- High modulus allows faster energy transfer from rider to frame <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Enhances responsiveness and efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Allows fine-tuned design for flex in specific frame zones <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Supports aerodynamic and weight-reduction goals without compromising strength <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per appropriate comparison or explanation up to [6 max]*

questions.specification:
What mechanical property is being tested here?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/205f4c8e-a9b1-4cb4-8842-cac78850e19b.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Tensile strength is resistance to being pulled apart (tension), typically measured using a tensile test rather than a three-point bend setup.

Bending strength (or flexural strength) is the ability of a material to resist deformation under a load. The image shows a three-point bend test, which is a standard method for measuring this mechanical property.
Toughness is the ability to absorb energy before fracturing, often assessed via impact tests (e.g. Charpy/Izod), not a three-point bend strength test.
Hardness is resistance to indentation or scratching, measured using indentation methods (e.g. Vickers, Rockwell), not a three-point bending test.

questions.specification:
Identify the type of structure shown in the image of the bicycle component.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/14f11189-5ddd-4861-93ee-28287eee9c2f.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Solid structures are solid throughout or have a very large mass compared to any hollow spaces, such as a brick or a hammer head.
Natural structures are those found in the natural world, such as a human skeleton or a bird's nest, rather than man-made components.
A bicycle frame is a skeleton of members (tubes) joined together to support a load, which is the defining characteristic of a frame structure.
While a lattice involves intersecting members, 'frame structure' is the primary classification used for structures like bicycle frames in Design Technology curriculum.

questions.specification: In structural design, what is the role of applying a strengthening technique like triangulation?

options.content: To convert tensile stress into shear stress
To reduce deformation and increase stability
To distribute loads more effectively through rigid triangular forms
To reduce bending moments by providing additional bracing and stiffness

questions.specification: A structure is in equilibrium. Which of the following is true?

options.content: The structure is experiencing elastic deformation
The resultant force and the resultant torque are both zero
All external forces acting on the structure are zero

questions.specification: Which of the following pairs provides examples of a structure found in both nature and the built environment?

options.content: A tree branch and a bridge truss
options.markscheme: Both a tree branch and a bridge truss are examples of frame structures. Frame structures use a network of members to support loads and are found in both natural (e.g., skeletons, trees) and built (e.g., cranes, trusses) environments.

options.content: A fish scale and a solar panel
options.markscheme: While biomimicry might involve patterns from fish scales in some technologies, they do not represent the same fundamental structural classification (mass, frame, or shell) in this context.

options.content: A car engine and a bird feather
options.markscheme: A car engine is a complex mechanical system, and while a feather has structural properties, they are not grouped together as a common structural type.

options.content: A water droplet and a resistor
options.markscheme: A water droplet is held by surface tension and does not constitute a solid load-bearing structure like a resistor, which is an electronic component.

questions.specification: A design consultancy is working with an urban infrastructure company to improve the safety and durability of public structures. As part of the project, the team is analyzing a range of real-world examples to evaluate structural types, stress behaviours, and strengthening strategies.

parts.content:
The team begins by examining a steel truss bridge located in a busy city district.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9970196f-5a48-4b6d-abd4-0c9adc33ac05)

i.) Identify the type of structural system shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Frame structure <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for correctly naming “frame” as the structural system.*

parts.content: ii.) State one reason why a truss bridge typically has a high safety factor.
parts.markscheme:
To ensure the bridge can withstand loads beyond maximum expected traffic, such as during emergencies <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for a clear explanation of the purpose of high SF in truss structures.*

parts.content:
Next, the team evaluates a suspension pedestrian bridge made of composite cables and anchored beams.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/384aa2b9-fe9a-44fa-93a3-efdead3e3585)

Explain how tension and compression forces act in the suspension cables and supporting pillars of **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
Suspension cables are under tension as they carry the vertical load from the bridge deck <marks>1 mark</marks>

Supporting pillars are under compression as they counter the vertical force pulling down from the cables <marks>1 mark</marks>

The opposing forces provide structural stability and distribute the load efficiently <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award \[1\] for each correct force description related to cable tension, pillar compression, and the interaction between them.*

parts.content:
The final structure under review is a modern stadium roof constructed using a grid shell system made of laminated timber beams.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d14cc173-ddf7-4651-a5df-bacc7dbf552b)
i.) Describe how lamination contributes to the structural integrity of the stadium roof.
parts.markscheme:
Increases bending strength by combining layers of wood with alternating grain directions <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduces the risk of fracture or splitting under dynamic wind loads <marks>1 mark</marks>

Enhances stiffness, allowing the roof to span wide distances with fewer supports <marks>1 mark</marks>

Improves uniformity and control over material defects <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award up to \[4\] for relevant benefits of lamination in large-span structural applications.*

parts.content: ii.) Compare the advantages of using a shell structure like the stadium roof with a frame structure like the truss bridge.
parts.markscheme:
Shell structures are often lighter and more efficient in distributing loads through surface geometry <marks>1 mark</marks>

Frame structures are typically easier to construct and modify due to modular components <marks>1 mark</marks>

Shell structures can resist forces in multiple directions through curved forms, reducing stress concentrations <marks>1 mark</marks>

Frame structures are better for standardised manufacturing and transport <marks>1 mark</marks>

Shells can be aesthetically striking but are complex to design; frames offer more design flexibility <marks>1 mark</marks>

Overall, shells optimise material use in long-span designs, while frames offer simpler construction and repair <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-developed comparison, up to [6].*

questions.specification: What structural feature is used to ensure a building sways safely during an earthquake?

parts.content: Identify two natural structures and two human-made structures.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify two natural structures (e.g. tree, coral reef) <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify two human-made structures (e.g. bridge, skyscraper) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Do not award both marks for two of the same category.</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following techniques would best improve the compressive strength of a column?

options.content: Adding internal ribbing or external buttresses
options.markscheme: Adding internal ribbing or external buttresses increases the cross-sectional area and improves the distribution of material, which enhances the column's ability to resist axial loads and buckling.
Reflectivity is a surface property related to electromagnetic radiation and does not affect the mechanical load-bearing capacity of a structure.

While reducing height can improve resistance to buckling (slenderness ratio), it is a change in the structural dimensions rather than a design technique applied to reinforce a column of a given specification.
Plastic coatings are generally used for finishing, aesthetics, or corrosion resistance and do not contribute to the structural compressive strength.

questions.specification: Which structural material would likely be chosen for a springboard due to its high elasticity?

options.markscheme: Glass-fibre composites can flex and are used in many sporting applications, but they generally have lower stiffness and energy return than carbon-fibre for a high-performance springboard.
Carbon-fibre composites have a high strength-to-weight ratio and excellent elastic properties, allowing them to store and release energy efficiently in a springboard.
While steel is elastic, its high density makes it less ideal than composites for the lightweight, responsive design required for most springboards.
Aluminium alloys are used for some springboards, but carbon-fibre is generally preferred in high-performance applications where superior elasticity and fatigue resistance are critical.

questions.specification: When selecting a material for a bridge cable under tension, which property is most important to minimise extension (stretching) under load?

options.markscheme: Correct: a higher Young’s modulus means the cable is stiffer and extends less for a given load.
Incorrect: flexibility relates to ease of bending, not directly to minimising axial extension under tensile load.
Incorrect: tensile strength is important to avoid failure, but it does not determine extension under load in the elastic region.
Incorrect: ductility is the ability to plastically deform before fracture; it is not the key property for minimising elastic extension.

questions.specification: What best describes structural redundancy in engineering?

options.content: Extra elements added to prevent collapse in case of failure
The reduction of total load through shape optimization
The act of mirroring a design for symmetry
A material’s ability to return to shape

questions.specification:
Which technique is used to strengthen the structure shown in the image?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0828a31b-33df-4be1-9d97-45141a455266)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A3.2 Introduction to structural systems (HL only) > A3.2.8 Strengthening techniques in structures
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A3.2.8 Strengthening techniques in structures
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify a specific technique used to strengthen a structure from a set of options (corrugation, lamination, ribbing, etc.), which is the core focus of the leaf topic 'Strengthening techniques in structures'.

options.markscheme: Tapering involves reducing the thickness or width of an object toward one end. While it can be used for structural reasons, it is not the technique shown here for strengthening a sheet material.

Corrugation is a technique where a material is shaped into a series of parallel ridges and furrows (fluting). This significantly increases the material's stiffness and resistance to bending, especially when used as a core between two flat liners.
Lamination involves bonding multiple layers of material together to improve strength or properties, which differs from the fluted geometry of corrugation.
Ribbing refers to adding raised strips or 'ribs' to the surface of a component (common in plastic mouldings) to increase rigidity, but it is distinct from continuous corrugation.

questions.specification: What is a key purpose of gussets, ribs, or corrugation in a structure?
Why are aircraft frames designed to handle loads far greater than typical operating conditions?

options.content: To protect against unexpected stresses in use
To resist sudden impact during assembly

questions.specification: Which of the following best describes a safety factor of $2.5$ in structural engineering?

options.content: The structure will deform plastically before it breaks.
The structure can carry $2.5$ times the expected maximum load.
The load is applied at $2.5$ times the expected speed.
The structure will fail after $2.5$ cycles of use.

parts.content: Describe the role of compression and tension in a bridge structure.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** compression as the force that resists pushing or squeezing in structural elements such as pillars <marks>A1</marks>

- **Define** tension as the force that resists pulling or stretching in structural elements such as cables or beams <marks>A1</marks>

- **Apply** these forces to a specific bridge structure (e.g., in a suspension bridge, the weight of the deck is supported by tension in the cables and compression in the towers) <marks>M1</marks>

- **Explain** how the balance between these opposing forces is essential for maintaining the equilibrium and structural integrity of the bridge <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award only if contextually correct; diagrams not required but help.</note>

questions.specification: A design team is engineering three distinct architectural elements for a new eco-resort complex: a roof made from a compression arch, a staircase supported by a space frame, and an observation tower stabilized with cross-bracing. Each structural element has been selected for its unique performance under load and form.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a timber compression arch roof used to span an open dining hall.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/59b2dda7-0bf4-46f8-a2f7-e41057f0625d)

i.) Identify the primary structural force acting on the arch.
parts.markscheme:
Compression <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for correctly identifying compression as the dominant force.*

parts.content: ii.) State one advantage of using an arch form to span open spaces.
parts.markscheme:
Arches efficiently carry loads by redirecting vertical forces into horizontal thrust, allowing for wide, column-free interiors. <marks>2 marks</marks>

*Award [1] for the structural concept and [1] for the functional benefit.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a steel space frame structure supporting a floating staircase.

 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3265e39d-5955-4757-95e9-8f1f473b2d57)


i.) Outline two benefits of using a space frame in staircase support.
parts.markscheme:
Provides three-dimensional load distribution for enhanced rigidity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Enables visually light and open designs without sacrificing strength. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clear benefit up to [2 max].*

parts.content: ii.) Explain how space frames achieve structural stability through geometry.
parts.markscheme:
Space frames are composed of interconnected tetrahedral or triangular units that form a rigid lattice. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The geometry prevents deformation under multi-directional loading. <marks>1 mark</marks>

This results in high strength-to-weight ratios and structural redundancy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per relevant explanation up to [3 max].*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a steel observation tower stabilized by cross-bracing between vertical members.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a8d5dab3-4b87-4c1c-8cf4-f2afafaa1a64)

i.) Describe how cross-bracing enhances the structural performance of the tower.
parts.markscheme:
Cross-bracing resists lateral loads such as wind or seismic forces. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It reduces sway and increases stiffness by forming triangulated zones. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It allows taller structures to maintain vertical alignment under stress. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-explained point up to [3 max].*

parts.content: ii.) Compare the structural performance of the cross-braced tower with a tower using only vertical columns and horizontal beams.
parts.markscheme:
Cross-bracing improves resistance to lateral forces compared to basic post-and-beam systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Towers without bracing are more susceptible to buckling or sway under high loads. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Cross-bracing can reduce the need for additional material in thick columns. <marks>1 mark</marks>

However, braced systems may limit internal access or create aesthetic compromises. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Basic frame systems offer design simplicity but require greater structural mass. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Cross-bracing can be optimized to balance visual transparency with performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clear and well-developed comparison point up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: Why are safety factors used in structural design?

options.content: To increase the weight of the structure
To allow for unpredictable forces and material inconsistencies
To limit the use of high-cost materials

questions.specification:
Why are the steel beams in this skyscraper oversized compared to actual expected loads?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/6b63cb5f-c1bf-438d-8c8d-cafdd4a113f4.jpeg)

options.content: To lower costs on lighter materials
To withstand loads greater than expected (safety margin)

questions.specification: Which of the following scenarios involves dynamic loading?

options.content: A wall resisting a constant, steady air pressure
A parked car on a bridge
A train moving over a track

questions.specification:
What concept is most closely represented by this bridge collapse due to overloading?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfoB-nu5pmReT8oImuj_Vk5ZuaT-BKbeYWn8ViLjRB8HsLtdf4mkvSXbQSyKnnV0Dj_ndFQ7ygJQ3kCFQjYnXYntpDldgs515ThjZJwMn0bIY3B29x2qk9-pRu1RKMHCAxyFFRT8Q?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

A structural design consultancy is exploring how different materials and structures contribute to the performance, safety and environmental footprint of architectural and product-based designs. Refer to the following figures.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a pedestrian footbridge with curved laminated timber beams.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/107bd51e-4680-43a7-86cd-f83cec5ea6ac)

Refer to **Figure 1**. Identify the type of forces acting on the main beams of the footbridge and explain how the shape of the structure helps manage these forces.
parts.markscheme:
Compression and bending forces <marks>1 mark</marks>

Curved laminated beams distribute the load evenly, reducing peak stresses <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for identifying the forces, [1] for explanation linked to structural shape*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a composite sports helmet made from carbon fibre and expanded polystyrene (EPS).


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/23b768be-b38a-430d-9c27-a919a885b3a3)

Refer to **Figure 2**. Explain why composite materials are chosen for the helmet’s construction.
parts.markscheme:
Carbon fibre offers high strength and low weight; EPS provides impact absorption <marks>1 mark</marks>

Combines stiffness with energy dissipation to improve safety <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per correctly explained material benefit up to [2 max]*

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 2**. Define the term “yield strength” and explain why it is important when designing safety equipment like helmets.
parts.markscheme:
Yield strength: stress at which a material begins to deform permanently <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ensures outer shell resists deformation to protect the inner impact-absorbing layer <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award [1] for accurate definition, [1] for relevant applied explanation

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows interconnected bamboo geodesic domes used for outdoor classrooms.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/89b77a5b-2621-4831-a67b-09c50a29dba1)

Refer to **Figure 3**. Identify the type of structural system used and outline two advantages of this structure in a sustainable design context.
parts.markscheme:
Geodesic frame (shell structure) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Efficient load distribution allows minimal use of material <marks>1 mark</marks>

Bamboo is renewable and lightweight <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for identification, [1] per relevant advantage up to [3 max]*

parts.content: Refer to **Figures 1 and 3**. Compare how the materials used in each structure affect their behaviour under dynamic environmental conditions (e.g. wind, moisture).
parts.markscheme:
Laminated timber can swell/shrink with moisture but is often sealed; high stiffness <marks>1 mark</marks>

Bamboo is naturally resilient but less consistent in properties; flexible in wind <marks>1 mark</marks>

Both materials require treatment for durability but offer sustainable alternatives to steel <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly explained comparison up to [3 max]*

parts.content: Refer to **all three figures**. Evaluate how structural design choices contribute to both functional performance and sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
Use of engineered wood and bamboo reduces environmental footprint <marks>1 mark</marks>

Geodesic design and curvature enhance load distribution, allowing material savings <marks>1 mark</marks>

Composite structures in helmets improve impact resistance without added weight <marks>1 mark</marks>

Natural materials need more maintenance or protection but support eco-design goals <marks>1 mark</marks>

Functionality (load-bearing, impact absorption) is preserved or enhanced while reducing material input <marks>1 mark</marks>

Structural form (curves, domes, layering) often aids both performance and material efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-explained point up to [6 max]*

parts.content: Explain the concept of equilibrium in structural systems and identify when failure may occur.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** equilibrium as the state where all forces and moments acting on a structural system are balanced <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that stability in a structural system depends on the absence of any net force or net moment <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** that failure occurs when the applied forces exceed the material strength or when the system becomes unbalanced <marks>A1</marks>
- **Give** a relevant example of failure, such as a collapsing column or buckling <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award only if both stability and failure are clearly addressed.</note>

questions.specification: A structure uses curved arches instead of straight beams. What is the most likely reason?

options.content: To increase the tensile strength of the structure
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Arches primarily keep loads in compression and reduce tensile stresses; they do not mainly function by increasing tensile strength.
Incorrect. Arches are generally more complex to design and construct than simple straight beams, often requiring temporary scaffolding (centering).

options.content: To redirect compressive forces and improve stability
options.markscheme: Correct. Arches work by resolving vertical loads into compressive forces along the curve of the arch, which are then transferred to the ground or supports, enhancing structural stability.
Incorrect. While arches (like domes) can reflect sound, this is an architectural or acoustic secondary effect rather than the primary structural reason for choosing an arch over a beam.

topic.title: A3.2 Introduction to structural systems (HL only)
